question: [File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: CALLBACK_SUCCESS / take()] Can an unprivileged taker acting as a sell-offer maker deploy a contract that implements ISellCallback and returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS from onSell() while reentering withdraw() on the seller's credit position under preconditions where the seller has nonzero credit and the outer take() has already updated sellerPos.credit but not yet executed the health check, triggering the call sequence take(sell_offer, ..., sellerCallback=attacker) -> onSell() -> withdraw(market, units, seller, attacker), violating the invariant that credit withdrawal must not occur before the post-take health check at Midnight.sol:476, causing scoped impact: unauthorized withdrawal of loan tokens from the seller's credit position? Proof idea: unit test with a malicious ISellCallback that reenters withdraw() and assert that the seller's credit balance decreases beyond what the health check would permit.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: CALLBACK_SUCCESS / take()] Can an unprivileged taker deploy a malicious IBuyCallback that returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS from onBuy() while reentering withdrawCollateral() on the seller's collateral under preconditions where the seller has debt and the outer take() has set LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT for (id, seller) but the health check has not yet run, triggering the call sequence take(buy_offer, ..., buyerCallback=attacker) -> onBuy() -> withdrawCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, seller, attacker), violating the invariant that collateral cannot be withdrawn if it would make debt unhealthy, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure from the seller's position? Proof idea: fuzz collateral amounts and assert isHealthy() returns false after the reentrant withdrawCollateral() but the transaction does not revert.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: CALLBACK_SUCCESS / repay()] Can an unprivileged operator authorized over a borrower deploy a malicious IRepayCallback that returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS from onRepay() while reentering repay() a second time with the same units under preconditions where the borrower has debt >= 2*units and the outer repay() has already decremented position[id][onBehalf].debt by units but not yet pulled tokens, triggering the call sequence repay(market, units, borrower, callback, data) -> onRepay() -> repay(market, units, borrower, address(0), '') -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, callback, this, units) [outer], violating the invariant that every debt reduction must correspond to an equal token inflow, causing scoped impact: unauthorized debt reduction by 2*units with only units tokens transferred? Proof idea: invariant test asserting position.debt decrease equals token balance increase after each repay call.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: CALLBACK_SUCCESS / liquidate()] Can an unprivileged liquidator deploy a malicious ILiquidateCallback that returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS from onLiquidate() while reentering liquidate() a second time on the same borrower under preconditions where the borrower has debt > maxDebt and multiple collateral assets, triggering the call sequence liquidate(market, collatIdx, seizedAssets, 0, borrower, false, receiver, callback, data) -> safeTransfer(collateral, receiver, seizedAssets) -> onLiquidate() -> liquidate(market, collatIdx2, seizedAssets2, 0, borrower, false, receiver, address(0), '') -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, callback, this, repaidUnits) [outer], violating the invariant that repay amount and seize amount must be mutually consistent and bounded by the RCF, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure exceeding the recovery close factor? Proof idea: assert total seized collateral value divided by total repaid units exceeds maxLif after both liquidation calls.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: CALLBACK_SUCCESS / flashLoan()] Can an unprivileged attacker deploy a malicious IFlashLoanCallback that returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS from onFlashLoan() while reentering take() with a sell offer to borrow loan tokens under preconditions where the attacker has supplied collateral and the flash loan provides the tokens needed as payer, triggering the call sequence flashLoan([loanToken], [assets], callback, data) -> safeTransfer(loanToken, callback, assets) -> onFlashLoan() -> take(sell_offer, ..., takerCallback=address(0)) [attacker borrows] -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, callback, this, assets) [flash repay], violating the invariant that every debt increase must pass collateral and gate checks with the borrower's own collateral, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing using flash-loaned tokens as collateral substitute? Proof idea: assert that after the flash loan the attacker holds debt units without having deposited collateral of sufficient value.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT / UtilsLib.tExchange() / take()] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the transient storage slot computed as keccak256(abi.encode(id, seller, LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT)) by crafting a (id2, seller2) pair that hashes to the same slot under preconditions where a take() is in progress for (id, seller) and the attacker controls a callback, triggering the call sequence take(offer_for_id_seller, ..., buyerCallback=attacker) -> onBuy() -> liquidate(market2, ..., seller2, ...) [where slot(id2,seller2)==slot(id,seller)], violating the invariant that liquidationLocked(id, seller) must be true during all callbacks of take(), causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure of the locked seller via a slot-collision liquidation? Proof idea: brute-force or birthday-attack search for (id2, seller2) pairs that produce the same keccak256 slot and assert liquidationLocked(id, seller) returns false during the callback.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT / take() / liquidate()] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate() on a seller who is currently inside a take() callback under preconditions where the liquidator uses a different market id (id2 != id) for the same seller address, triggering the call sequence [tx1: take(offer_id, ..., sellerCallback=attacker)] -> onSell() -> [tx2 or reentrant: liquidate(market2, ..., seller, ...)] where liquidationLocked(id2, seller) is false because the lock is only set for (id, seller), violating the invariant that the seller cannot be liquidated during the callbacks of take(), causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure of the seller in a different market while their position is partially updated? Proof idea: deploy a sell callback that reenters liquidate() with a different market id and assert the liquidation succeeds.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT / take() reentrance] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the wasLocked flag in take() by triggering a reentrant take() for the same (id, seller) from a buy callback under preconditions where the outer take() has set wasLocked=false and the inner take() sets wasLocked=true (already locked), triggering the call sequence take(outer, buy_offer, ..., buyerCallback=attacker) -> onBuy() -> take(inner, sell_offer, same_seller, ...) [inner sets wasLocked=true, does not clear lock] -> [inner completes, lock remains set] -> [outer completes, clears lock since wasLocked=false], violating the invariant that the seller's health check must occur while the lock is still active, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing by the seller in the inner take() without a valid health check? Proof idea: assert that after both takes complete, the seller's debt exceeds their maxDebt.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: isAuthorized / CALLBACK_SUCCESS / setIsAuthorized()] Can an unprivileged callback receiver that was authorized by a victim call setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, victim) from within a ISellCallback.onSell() or IBuyCallback.onBuy() callback under preconditions where the victim has authorized the callback contract for a limited purpose (e.g., rolling positions), triggering the call sequence take(offer, ..., sellerCallback=callback) -> onSell() -> setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, victim) [callback is authorized so this succeeds] -> [later tx] withdrawCollateral(market, idx, assets, victim, attacker), violating the invariant that authorization must only allow intended account delegation, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure via escalated authorization? Proof idea: state test asserting isAuthorized[victim][attacker] becomes true after the callback and the attacker can subsequently drain collateral.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: isAuthorized / setIsAuthorized() / multicall()] Can an unprivileged authorized operator use multicall() to atomically call setIsAuthorized(newAttacker, true, victim) followed by withdrawCollateral(market, idx, assets, victim, attacker) under preconditions where the operator is authorized over the victim and the victim has collateral with no debt, triggering the call sequence multicall([setIsAuthorized(newAttacker, true, victim), withdrawCollateral(market, idx, assets, victim, attacker)]), violating the invariant that authorized addresses must not be able to grant further authorizations that exceed the original scope, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure via coarse-grained authorization chaining? Proof idea: unit test asserting that a single multicall can both escalate authorization and drain collateral in one atomic transaction.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: isAuthorized[offer.maker][offer.ratifier] / take()] Can an unprivileged attacker who has been authorized by a maker (isAuthorized[maker][attacker] == true) deploy a ratifier contract they control and use it to ratify offers with arbitrary parameters under preconditions where the maker has authorized the attacker for a different purpose (e.g., position management), triggering the call sequence [attacker deploys MaliciousRatifier that always returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS] -> take(offer{maker=victim, ratifier=MaliciousRatifier}, ...) [passes because isAuthorized[victim][MaliciousRatifier] is false] -- OR -- [attacker calls setIsAuthorized(MaliciousRatifier, true, victim) if already authorized] -> take(offer{maker=victim, ratifier=MaliciousRatifier}, ...), violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind correct maker, market, offer, amount, price, deadline, nonce, salt, and consumption group, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing via ratifier substitution? Proof idea: assert that a take() with a malicious ratifier succeeds when isAuthorized[maker][maliciousRatifier] is true.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified() / CALLBACK_SUCCESS] Can an attacker supply a Merkle proof in ratifierData where leafIndex is crafted to place the offer hash at an internal node of the tree rather than a leaf under preconditions where HashLib.isLeaf() does not distinguish leaf nodes from internal nodes, triggering the call sequence take(offer, ratifierData={sig, root, leafIndex=internal_node_idx, proof}, units, ...) -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified() -> HashLib.isLeaf(root, hashOffer(offer), leafIndex, proof) [passes if internal node matches] -> returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the exact offer hash as a leaf, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing via forged Merkle proof that passes a non-leaf node as a valid offer? Proof idea: construct a Merkle tree where an internal node equals the hash of a crafted offer and assert isRatified() returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified() / ecrecover / CALLBACK_SUCCESS] Can an attacker supply a signature (v, r, s) where ecrecover returns address(0) under preconditions where isAuthorized[offer.maker][address(0)] is true (maker has authorized the zero address, e.g., by mistake or via a callback), triggering the call sequence take(offer, ratifierData={sig_with_zero_signer, root, leafIndex, proof}, ...) -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified() -> ecrecover returns address(0) -> require(_signer != address(0)) REVERTS, violating -- OR -- if the zero-address check is absent in a custom ratifier, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing via zero-address ecrecover bypass? Proof idea: fuzz (v, r, s) values that cause ecrecover to return address(0) and test against any ratifier that omits the zero-address check.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot() / isAuthorized / consumed] Can an unprivileged authorized operator call EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(maker, root) to cancel a valid Merkle root, then call setConsumed(group, 0, maker) -- wait, setConsumed requires amount >= consumed -- under preconditions where the operator is authorized over the maker and the maker has active offers under that root, triggering the call sequence [operator calls cancelRoot(maker, root)] -> [maker's offers under root are invalidated] -> [operator calls setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker) to cancel group budget] -> [maker cannot fill any offers], violating the invariant that offer cancellation must only be performed by the maker or their intended delegate, causing scoped impact: unauthorized debt reduction via forced offer cancellation that prevents the maker from closing positions? Proof idea: assert that after cancelRoot() the maker's valid signed offers all revert with RootCanceled.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: isAuthorized / take() / offer.ratifier] Can an unprivileged attacker who controls a contract that is both a ratifier and a callback receiver use the callback execution context to observe partial state (e.g., updated consumed amounts) and ratify a second offer that would not have been ratified in isolation under preconditions where the attacker's contract is authorized as the ratifier for the maker's offers, triggering the call sequence take(offer1, ..., ratifier=attacker_contract) -> attacker_contract.isRatified() [observes consumed[maker][group]] -> returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS -> take(offer2, ..., ratifier=attacker_contract) [from within a callback, using observed state to craft ratifierData], violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind correct consumed state at the time of ratification, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing via state-dependent ratifier that exploits partial execution order? Proof idea: deploy a ratifier that reads consumed state during isRatified() and assert it can approve offers that exceed the maker's intended budget.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: IEnterGate.canIncreaseDebt() / CALLBACK_SUCCESS / take()] Can an unprivileged taker bypass the enter gate's canIncreaseDebt() check by using a IRepayCallback that reenters take() with a sell offer under preconditions where the enter gate blocks the taker from increasing debt directly but the repay callback is not subject to the gate check, triggering the call sequence repay(market, 0, taker, repayCallback, data) [0 units, no-op debt reduction] -> onRepay() -> take(sell_offer, ...) [sellerDebtIncrease > 0, gate check: canIncreaseDebt(taker)] -- gate is called but with the same taker -- OR -- repay with nonzero units to first reduce debt then re-borrow via callback, violating the invariant that every debt increase must pass gate checks, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing bypassing the enter gate? Proof idea: deploy a gate that blocks canIncreaseDebt() for the taker and assert that the repay-callback-reenter-take sequence succeeds.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: IEnterGate.canIncreaseCredit() / IBuyCallback / take()] Can an unprivileged buyer bypass the enter gate's canIncreaseCredit() check by routing through a IBuyCallback that calls take() with a buy offer on behalf of a different buyer address under preconditions where the gate blocks the original buyer but not the callback contract, triggering the call sequence take(buy_offer{buyer=blocked_user}, ..., buyerCallback=attacker) -> onBuy() -> take(buy_offer2{buyer=attacker_contract}, ...) [gate checks canIncreaseCredit(attacker_contract) which returns true], violating the invariant that gate checks must apply to all entry paths for credit increase, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing (credit increase) bypassing the enter gate via callback indirection? Proof idea: deploy a gate that blocks specific addresses and assert that a callback-mediated take() for a blocked address succeeds.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: ILiquidatorGate.canLiquidate() / isAuthorized / liquidate()] Can an unprivileged attacker bypass the liquidator gate by calling liquidate() through an authorized operator address that the gate approves under preconditions where the liquidator gate checks msg.sender (the authorized operator) rather than the original caller, and the attacker has obtained authorization from an approved operator, triggering the call sequence [attacker obtains isAuthorized[approvedOperator][attacker] == true] -> [attacker calls liquidate() directly -- but msg.sender is attacker, not approvedOperator] -- OR -- [attacker deploys a contract that is authorized by approvedOperator and calls liquidate() with msg.sender=attacker_contract], violating the invariant that the liquidator gate must not be bypassed through alternate liquidation or callback paths, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure via liquidator gate bypass? Proof idea: deploy a liquidator gate that checks msg.sender and assert that an unauthorized address can liquidate by routing through an authorized intermediary.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: ILiquidatorGate.canLiquidate() / ILiquidateCallback / liquidate()] Can an unprivileged attacker bypass the liquidator gate by using a ILiquidateCallback that calls liquidate() on a different borrower under preconditions where the outer liquidate() passes the gate check (attacker is approved) but the inner liquidate() is called with a different borrower and the gate is not re-checked for the inner call, triggering the call sequence liquidate(market, ..., borrower1, ..., callback=attacker) -> onLiquidate() -> liquidate(market, ..., borrower2, ..., address(0), '') [no gate check bypass since msg.sender is still attacker], violating -- OR -- where the gate is stateful and the outer call changes gate state allowing the inner call, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure of borrower2 via callback-mediated liquidation? Proof idea: deploy a stateful liquidator gate and assert that the callback can trigger a second liquidation that the gate would have blocked.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: IEnterGate / take() / multicall()] Can an unprivileged taker use multicall() to batch a take() that reduces existing debt (sellerDebtIncrease == 0, sellerCreditDecrease > 0) followed by a take() that increases debt (sellerDebtIncrease > 0) under preconditions where the enter gate's canIncreaseDebt() is stateful and the first take changes the gate's state to allow the second take, triggering the call sequence multicall([take(sell_offer1, units1) [reduces credit, no gate check], take(sell_offer2, units2) [increases debt, gate now returns true]]), violating the invariant that gate checks must apply to all debt-increasing entry paths regardless of prior calls in the same transaction, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing via multicall gate state manipulation? Proof idea: deploy a stateful gate that tracks credit/debt changes and assert that the multicall sequence bypasses the gate.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: CALLBACK_SUCCESS / repay() / take()] Can an unprivileged attacker use a IRepayCallback that reenters take() with a sell offer to re-borrow the same units that were just repaid under preconditions where the repay() has already decremented position[id][onBehalf].debt and incremented marketState[id].withdrawable but not yet pulled tokens, triggering the call sequence repay(market, units, borrower, callback, data) [debt -= units, withdrawable += units] -> onRepay() -> take(sell_offer, units, borrower, ...) [debt += units again, gate check passes since debt was just reduced] -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, callback, this, units) [repay token pull], violating the invariant that every debt increase must correspond to a net new obligation, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing by re-increasing debt that was just reduced without net token outflow? Proof idea: assert that after the repay+callback+take sequence the borrower's debt equals the original value but the protocol received no net tokens.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: CALLBACK_SUCCESS / take() / isHealthy()] Can an unprivileged attacker use a ISellCallback that reenters supplyCollateral() to temporarily satisfy the health check at Midnight.sol:476 and then immediately withdraws the collateral in a subsequent call under preconditions where the seller's position is unhealthy after the take but the callback supplies enough collateral to pass isHealthy(), triggering the call sequence take(sell_offer, ..., sellerCallback=attacker) -> onSell() -> supplyCollateral(market, idx, assets, seller) [health check will pass] -> [take completes, isHealthy() passes] -> [next tx] withdrawCollateral(market, idx, assets, seller, attacker), violating the invariant that the health check must reflect the borrower's true collateral position at settlement, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing with transiently supplied collateral that is immediately withdrawn? Proof idea: assert that after the full sequence the seller has debt exceeding their maxDebt with no collateral.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: LLTV_8 = WAD / maxLif() / liquidate()] Can an unprivileged liquidator in a market with LLTV_8 = WAD (maxLif = WAD, maxRepaid = type(uint256).max) call liquidate() with repaidUnits set to the full debt amount even when the position is only 1 wei unhealthy under preconditions where the RCF is deactivated (lltv == WAD sets maxRepaid = type(uint256).max at Midnight.sol:661), triggering the call sequence liquidate(market{lltv=WAD}, collatIdx, 0, position.debt, borrower, false, receiver, address(0), ''), violating the invariant that repay amount must be capped by what is needed to restore health (RCF), causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure of the entire position for a minimally unhealthy borrower in a LLTV=1 market? Proof idea: create a LLTV=WAD market, make a position 1 wei unhealthy, and assert that full liquidation succeeds.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: TIME_TO_MAX_LIF = 15 minutes / liquidate() postMaturityMode] Can an unprivileged liquidator use a ILiquidateCallback to delay execution within the 15-minute TIME_TO_MAX_LIF window to obtain a higher LIF and seize more collateral than the debt warrants under preconditions where the market is post-maturity and the LIF ramps linearly from WAD to maxLif over 15 minutes, triggering the call sequence liquidate(market, collatIdx, seizedAssets, 0, borrower, true, receiver, callback, data) [at maturity+1s, lif≈WAD] -> onLiquidate() [callback delays via external call or loop until maturity+15min] -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, callback, this, repaidUnits) [repaidUnits computed at maturity+1s but lif is now maxLif], violating the invariant that seizedAssets and repaidUnits must be computed at the same block.timestamp, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure exceeding the fair LIF-adjusted value? Proof idea: assert that seizedAssets/repaidUnits ratio exceeds the LIF computed at the start of the liquidate() call.

[File: 'File Name: src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: maxLif() / LIQUIDATION_CURSOR_LOW / LIQUIDATION_CURSOR_HIGH / touchMarket()] Can an unprivileged market creator pass a CollateralParams.maxLif value that is 1 wei different from the value computed by maxLif(lltv, cursor) due to integer rounding in mulDivDown(WAD, WAD, WAD - mulDivDown(cursor, WAD - lltv, WAD)) under preconditions where the require at Midnight.sol:768-770 checks equality against both cursor values, triggering the call sequence touchMarket(market{collateralParams[0].maxLif = maxLif(lltv, LIQUIDATION_CURSOR_LOW) + 1}) [reverts with InvalidMaxLif] -- OR -- where the computed maxLif rounds such that the stored value differs from what liquidate() uses, violating the invariant that the LIF used in liquidation must match the validated maxLif, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure via a maxLif that exceeds the protocol-validated bound? Proof idea: fuzz all 9 LLTV values with both cursor values and assert maxLif() is deterministic and the equality check always passes.
